story:
On a muggy, honeysuckle-soaked morning, Jenny May ignored the one rule Clara—the only witch she knew and the owner of the town coffee shop—had drilled into her: don’t practice alone. Beginner’s Charms for the Young Witch gleamed on her desk anyway. Rules were suggestions, right? She whispered to her emerald snake, Viper, that it was only a color charm. “Rosalia Transformis,” she said.

The air thickened, lights flickered, Viper hissed—and with a pop became a small, fuzzy, very pink cat. It blinked turquoise eyes, meowed, and then drifted up like a lazy balloon, paws dangling toward the ceiling fan.

Daniel knocked at her window right on cue. He climbed in, saw the floating neon cotton candy with whiskers, and choked out, “You turned your snake into a cat?” Jenny, mortified: “A flying pink cat.” The reversal spell, she discovered, wanted moonlight, grave dust, and “a mirror older than your mother.” Not helpful at breakfast.

After a short while, A crash sent them racing to the kitchen, where Viper—still pink and airborne—batted at the wind chime until his fur sparkled brighter. He zip-lined out the back door and into sunlight. In the garden, a butterfly landed on his nose. He sneezed; the butterfly turned pink too.

“Oh no,” Jenny breathed. “He’s contagious.”

Daniel wheezed, “You’ve created a plague of pink.”

They chased Viper past tomatoes, over a fence, down the gravel road. He hovered near a streetlight, purring like a soft engine. Desperate, Jenny tried an unbinding charm. The shimmer dimmed—then split. Now there were two flying pink cats. A jogger stopped dead to gawk. Daniel squeezed her shoulder. “You’ll fix it. You always do.” Jenny winced. “Clara’s going to kill me.”

“Better furious than a glitter storm over town,” Daniel said. So they ran to Clara’s Coffee Shop, bells chiming, cinnamon and espresso wrapping around them like a spell. Clara took one look at Jenny’s pink-glowing aura and sighed. “What did you do?” Outside, two candyfloss cats looped the sky. “Oh, mercy,” Clara murmured.

“I can’t undo it,” Jenny admitted, voice cracking. Clara’s face softened. “Of course you can’t. Not alone.” She held out her hand for the book, flipped to the charm, and tapped the ink with a knowing finger. “You skipped the grounding verse. Magic needs roots, girl. Without them, it floats—like your cats.”

“I just wanted to make him pretty,” Jenny said, cheeks burning.

“Intentions are kind, but power is wild. It listens best to calm hands,” Clara said, eyes flashing silver as she whispered the missing lines. The air rippled. The twin cats twined once, then melted into a single, glowing shape. With a soft pop, Viper lay coiled in the grass again—his normal glossy green, tongue flicking as if none of this had ever happened.

“You did it,” Jenny breathed.

“Next time,” Clara said, handing back the book, “wait until your mentor’s had her coffee.”

“Maybe stick to goldfish,” Daniel added. Jenny glared, then laughed. She scooped up Viper. “Sorry, buddy. No more experiments.” On the walk home, Daniel nudged her. “Admit it. You kinda liked the flying pink version.”

“Maybe a little,” she said, watching faint sparkles still linger in the morning sky. “Still—let’s not test gravity again anytime soon.”

“Deal,” Daniel said. Viper hissed in agreement—or, Jenny thought, maybe it sounded a little like a purr.
